item.title: Slowly but surely, resistance to nuclear energy is melting away
item.description: Minnesota takes a step toward breaking its nuclear ban.

item.title: The evolution of cookbooks
item.description: Edith Pritchett cartoon on how cooking has changed.

item.title: Tom Holland sees echoes of Rome in the American experiment
item.description: A conversation with the British author, podcaster and amateur cricketer on the United States at 250.

item.title: 25 reasons to be optimistic about America’s future
item.description: Freedom and prosperity are never guaranteed, but it’s a mistake to bet against America.

item.title: The realities of parenting
item.description: Edith Pritchett cartoon on raising an infant.

item.title: George Washington failed before he succeeded
item.description: Before he became the great leader he is known to be, Washington faced a steep learning curve.

item.title: A $2.8 billion data center was kept secret. Now residents are furious.
item.description: The South Carolina development will hurt the environment and create few jobs.

item.title: Why Europeans are falling in love with America
item.description:
As European tourists travel across the United States for World Cup matches, many are discovering an America they didn’t expect: vast landscapes, friendly strangers, huge portions, small-town surprises and a sense of abundance that Americans often take for granted. Their reactions are going viral, offering a fresh reminder that the United States can look very different from the outside than it does on social media or the news.
Host Megan McArdle argues that Americans have become too pessimistic about their own country — and that seeing America through European eyes can help us rediscover what is still dynamic, generous, innovative and worth taking pride in.
